The **Diocese of Hong Kong** ([Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 天主教香港教區; [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Dioecesis Sciiamchiamensis*) is a [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") [diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"). The [diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") takes its name from the [see city](/wiki/Episcopal_see "Episcopal see"), the [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") where the bishop resides.

The Diocese of Hong Kong is a *de jure* [suffragan diocese](/wiki/Suffragan_diocese "Suffragan diocese") of the [Archdiocese of Guangzhou](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Guangzhou "Archdiocese of Guangzhou") (Canton).[[1]](#cite_note-1) However, in practice it is an immediate subject of the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Also in theory, not only Hong Kong, but also a small part of Guangdong province belongs[*[until when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] to the diocese. In practice, however, the diocese only comprises[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region.

There were about 395,000 local [Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") as of August 2022,[[2]](#cite_note-2) and 169,000 [Filipino](/wiki/Filipino_people "Filipino people") Catholics in Hong Kong.[[3]](#cite_note-statistics-3) Most of the Hong Kong Catholics are Chinese. However, there are various national groups of Filipino, Korean, Japanese, Indian, French and German active Catholics. They are served by 279 [priests](/wiki/Priest "Priest"), 36 [deacons](/wiki/Deacon "Deacon"), 62 [brothers](/wiki/Brother_(Christian) "Brother (Christian)") and 419 [sisters](/wiki/Nun "Nun").[[3]](#cite_note-statistics-3) There are 52 [parishes](/wiki/Parish "Parish"), comprising 39 [churches](/wiki/Church_(building) "Church (building)"), 28 [chapels](/wiki/Chapel "Chapel") and 24 halls for religious purposes including the celebration of the liturgy. As for education, in 2022 there are 249 Catholic schools and kindergartens,[[4]](#cite_note-4) having a total of over 136,000 pupils.

The [Bishop emeritus](/wiki/Bishop_emeritus "Bishop emeritus") of Hong Kong is [Joseph Cardinal Zen](/wiki/Joseph_Cardinal_Zen "Joseph Cardinal Zen") Ze-kiun, SDB (Chinese: 陳日君). He is regarded by some to be politically 'controversial' due to his '[anti-Beijing](/wiki/Anti-Beijing "Anti-Beijing")' views and his strong ties with the [pro-democracy camp](/wiki/Pro-democracy_camp_(Hong_Kong) "Pro-democracy camp (Hong Kong)"). However, Zen has constantly personally maintained that he is very patriotic to his country, and that he has been upset that he has been denied the right to return to [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China").

The [Bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") of Hong Kong is [Stephen Chow](/wiki/Stephen_Chow_(bishop) "Stephen Chow (bishop)"), [SJ](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits") (Chinese: 周守仁), who was appointed by [Pope Francis](/wiki/Pope_Francis "Pope Francis") in 2021.[[5]](#cite_note-5) He took over from [Cardinal John Tong Hon](/wiki/John_Tong_Hon "John Tong Hon"), who served as Apostolic Administrator following the death of [Michael Yeung Ming-cheung](/wiki/Michael_Yeung_Ming-cheung "Michael Yeung Ming-cheung") in 2019.

The cathedral of the Diocese of Hong Kong is the [Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Cathedral_of_the_Immaculate_Conception_(Hong_Kong) "Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception (Hong Kong)"), located on Caine Road, Mid Levels.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Hong_Kong&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The organisation of what would become the Diocese of Hong Kong began immediately after the establishment of Hong Kong as a [British colony](/wiki/British_colony "British colony").

### Prefecture apostolic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Hong_Kong&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Prefecture apostolic")]

In 1841 Pope [Gregory XVI](/wiki/Gregory_XVI "Gregory XVI") created a [prefecture apostolic](/wiki/Prefecture_apostolic "Prefecture apostolic")[[6]](#cite_note-Cordier-6) comprising "Hong Kong with the surrounding six [leagues](/wiki/League_(unit) "League (unit)")" independent from the [Diocese of Macau](/wiki/Diocese_of_Macau "Diocese of Macau"), but under the authority of the [bishop of Macau](/wiki/Bishop_of_Macau "Bishop of Macau"). Given the alarming rate of illness and death among British soldiers, the initial need for the establishment of the prefecture was the spiritual care of the British (Irish Catholic) soldiers stationed in the newly established colony.

[Theodore Joset](/wiki/Theodore_Joset "Theodore Joset"), a Swiss diocesan priest, and former [procurator](/wiki/Procurator_(Catholic_canon_law) "Procurator (Catholic canon law)") of the mission at Macau, became the first prefect apostolic.[[7]](#cite_note-chu-7) In 1842, at the junction of [Pottinger Street](/wiki/Pottinger_Street "Pottinger Street") and [Wellington Street](/wiki/Wellington_Street,_Hong_Kong "Wellington Street, Hong Kong"), he laid the foundation stone for the first Catholic church in Hong Kong.[[8]](#cite_note-8) It was dedicated to the [Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Immaculate_Conception "Immaculate Conception").

Following Joset's death in 1842, [Anthony Feliciani](/wiki/Anthony_Feliciani "Anthony Feliciani") became Prefect Apostolic of Hong Kong.[[9]](#cite_note-bdcc-9) The [St. Francis Xavier Chapel](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Mount_Carmel_Church_(Hong_Kong) "Our Lady of Mount Carmel Church (Hong Kong)"), for Catholic faithful in the [Wan Chai](/wiki/Wan_Chai "Wan Chai") area was founded in 1845.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Feliciani coordinated the mission work in China, and made substantial purchases of land in the [Starstreet Precinct](/wiki/Starstreet_Precinct "Starstreet Precinct"), the earliest burial ground assigned by the Hong Kong Government for the non-Chinese population.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The prefecture functioned much as a mission, but was intended from its inception to become a diocese. In the first ten years, the missionaries built churches, schools, a seminary, and institutions for the sick, elderly, and orphans. In response to the request of Prefect Apostolic [Théodore-Augustin Forcade](/wiki/Th%C3%A9odore-Augustin_Forcade "Théodore-Augustin Forcade"), four [Sisters of Saint Paul of Chartres](/wiki/Sisters_of_Saint_Paul_of_Chartres "Sisters of Saint Paul of Chartres") arrived in Hong Kong on 12 September 1848. The Sisters first task was to establish a home for unwanted babies, mostly girls, abandoned.[[11]](#cite_note-spc-11)

In 1858, the first missionaries, members of the Seminary of Foreign Missions of Milan (now [PIME](/wiki/PIME "PIME")) arrived; they were designated to take over the administration of the mission in time. They set about enlarging the cathedral, which, however, burnt down on 18 October 1859. It was rebuilt and blessed on 18 March 1860.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

By 1860, the physical territory had spread well beyond the initial six [leagues](/wiki/League_(unit) "League (unit)") surrounding Hong Kong to include the [San On District](/wiki/Bao%27an_County "Bao'an County") (新安縣), the [Kowloon Peninsula](/wiki/Kowloon_Peninsula "Kowloon Peninsula"), [Sai Kung Peninsula](/wiki/Sai_Kung_Peninsula "Sai Kung Peninsula"), and [Nam Tau](/wiki/Nantou,_Shenzhen_(historical) "Nantou, Shenzhen (historical)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Vicariate apostolic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Hong_Kong&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Vicariate apostolic")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/FileBuildings%2C_Hong_Kong._Photograph_by_John_Thomson%2C_1868-1871._Wellcome_L0055639_%28cropped_version_showing_cathedral%29.jpg/250px-FileBuildings%2C_Hong_Kong._Photograph_by_John_Thomson%2C_1868-1871._Wellcome_L0055639_%28cropped_version_showing_cathedral%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:FileBuildings,_Hong_Kong._Photograph_by_John_Thomson,_1868-1871._Wellcome_L0055639_(cropped_version_showing_cathedral).jpg)

Hong Kong Catholic cathedral, circa 1870.

In 1874 the Hong Kong Prefecture was raised to a [vicariate apostolic](/wiki/Vicariate_apostolic "Vicariate apostolic") and entrusted to the Seminary of Foreign Missions of Milan.[[6]](#cite_note-Cordier-6) While the prefecture had been run by missionary priests, a vicariate was the intermediary step before becoming a diocese, and required a bishop to run it. Since the territory was not yet a diocese, the bishops were called "titular bishop" of another place (where they had no ecclesiastical authority). The bishops were under the direct authority of the pope, exercising their power in his name, rather than being vested with the office belonging to a diocese.

The first vicar apostolic was Bishop Giovanni [Timoleon Raimondi](/wiki/Timoleon_Raimondi "Timoleon Raimondi") (consecrated 22 November 1874), who died at Mission House, [Glenealy](/wiki/Glenealy,_Hong_Kong "Glenealy, Hong Kong"), Hong Kong, on 27 September 1894. In 1880, the vicariate hosted the first [synod](/wiki/Synod "Synod") of the fifth ecclesiastical region of the Catholic Church in China; (it hosted a second synod in 1891, and a third in 1909). In 1883, the foundation stone of a new cathedral was laid. This is the present [Immaculate Conception Cathedral](/wiki/Immaculate_Conception_Cathedral_of_Hong_Kong "Immaculate Conception Cathedral of Hong Kong"). It was built in 1888 and consecrated in 1938.

Raimondi was succeeded by Bishops [Louis Piazzoli](/wiki/Louis_Piazzoli "Louis Piazzoli") (born 1849) and [Dominic Pozzoni](/wiki/Dominic_Pozzoni "Dominic Pozzoni") (born 1851) elected 26 May 1905. The vicariate continued to grow. In 1875, the [Brothers of the Christian Schools](/wiki/Brothers_of_the_Christian_Schools "Brothers of the Christian Schools") founded [St Joseph's College](/wiki/St_Joseph%27s_College,_Hong_Kong "St Joseph's College, Hong Kong"), Hong Kong’s oldest Catholic boys’ school.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The [Sisters of Canossa](/wiki/Canossians "Canossians") also established a presence in Hong Kong.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/HK_Garden_Road_37_St_Joseph_s_Catholic_Church_Carpark.JPG/250px-HK_Garden_Road_37_St_Joseph_s_Catholic_Church_Carpark.JPG)](/wiki/File:HK_Garden_Road_37_St_Joseph_s_Catholic_Church_Carpark.JPG)

St Joseph's Church, Garden Road

Besides [Hong Kong Island](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Island "Hong Kong Island"), the vicariate included Kowloon (which became part of the colony in 1860), [Lantau Island](/wiki/Lantau_Island "Lantau Island") (part of the colony since 1898) and the three continental districts of [San-on](/wiki/Bao%27an_County "Bao'an County") (新安), [Kwei Hsin](/w/index.php?title=Kwei_Hsin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kwei Hsin (page does not exist)") [[zh-tw](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%BD%92%E5%96%84%E5%8E%BF "zh-tw:归善县")] (歸善), and [Hoi Fung (Haï-fung)](/wiki/Hoi_Fung "Hoi Fung") (海豐).

Churches with resident priests were [the cathedral](/wiki/Cathedral_of_the_Immaculate_Conception_(Hong_Kong) "Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception (Hong Kong)") ([Glenealy](/wiki/Glenealy,_Hong_Kong "Glenealy, Hong Kong")), [St. Joseph's](/w/index.php?title=St._Joseph%27s_Church_(Hong_Kong)&action=edit&redlink=1 "St. Joseph's Church (Hong Kong) (page does not exist)") [[commons](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:St._Joseph%27s_Catholic_Church,_Hong_Kong "c:Category:St. Joseph's Catholic Church, Hong Kong")] ([Garden Road](/wiki/Garden_Road,_Hong_Kong "Garden Road, Hong Kong")), [St. Francis](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Mount_Carmel_Church_(Hong_Kong) "Our Lady of Mount Carmel Church (Hong Kong)") (Wan Chai), Church of the Sacred Heart ([West Point](/wiki/West_Point,_Hong_Kong "West Point, Hong Kong")), Church of St. Anthony[‌](/wiki/%E2%80%8C "‌") [[commons](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:HK_St_Anthony%27s_School.JPG "commons:File:HK St Anthony's School.JPG")] (West Point). The [Société des Missions Etrangères de Paris](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_des_Missions_Etrang%C3%A8res_de_Paris "Société des Missions Etrangères de Paris") had a procurator, a sanitorium ([Béthanie](/wiki/B%C3%A9thanie_(Hong_Kong) "Béthanie (Hong Kong)") in [Pok Fu Lam](/wiki/Pok_Fu_Lam "Pok Fu Lam")) and a printing office ([Nazareth](/wiki/University_Hall_(University_of_Hong_Kong) "University Hall (University of Hong Kong)")) at Hong Kong.

In 1875 the Société des Missions Etrangères de Paris established in Pok Fu Lam, [Béthanie](/wiki/B%C3%A9thanie_(Hong_Kong) "Béthanie (Hong Kong)") a sanitorium to serve as a place for priests and missionaries from all over Asia to recover from tropical diseases before returning to their missions. With the 1894, bubonic plague outbreak, much of the population left Hong Kong. [John Douglas Lapraik](/wiki/Douglas_Steamship_Company "Douglas Steamship Company") sold the family estate at Pok Fu Lam, Douglas Castle, to the French Mission. The building soon turned into a monastery and was renamed, Nazareth. The building went through a major renovation which included a printing house that operated one of the busiest bible printing and translation facilities of the early 20th century in Asia.

More missionaries arrived from many orders throughout the 1920s and 1930s, building more churches, schools, and hospitals. In 1935, the Dominicans established [St. Albert's Priory](/wiki/St._Albert_the_Great_Priory "St. Albert the Great Priory") (*aka*. St. Albert the Great's Priory), a House of Studies for the whole Far East, at the foot of [Mount Nicholson](/wiki/Mount_Nicholson "Mount Nicholson"), a place that came to be known as Rosary Hill. The priory closed and the students moved to the Philippines in 1959, and the premises have since been occupied by Rosaryhill School,[[13]](#cite_note-13) with part of the site becoming [Villa Monte Rosa](/w/index.php?title=Villa_Monte_Rosa&action=edit&redlink=1 "Villa Monte Rosa (page does not exist)") [[yue](https://zh-yue.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%8E%AB%E7%91%B0%E6%96%B0%E9%82%A8 "yue:玫瑰新邨")].

During World War II, the [Japanese occupation](/wiki/Japanese_occupation_of_Hong_Kong "Japanese occupation of Hong Kong") stopped almost all activities. Missionaries evacuated, and were variously interned, released, and expelled. After the war, reconstruction began immediately.

### Diocese

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Hong_Kong&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Diocese")]

On 11 April 1946 [Pope Pius XII](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XII "Pope Pius XII") established the episcopal hierarchy in China, raising all the [apostolic vicariates](/wiki/Apostolic_vicariate "Apostolic vicariate") to dioceses, Hong Kong among them. Since then, the Hong Kong Diocese is directly responsible to the pope. [Enrico Valtorta](/wiki/Enrico_Valtorta "Enrico Valtorta") became the first bishop of Hong Kong.

In 1949 refugees fleeing the Chinese communist regime began to pour into Hong Kong, including many Catholics and clergymen from all over China; diocesan activities were effectively restricted to the boundaries of the Colony. In 1952, the diocese opened seven new chapels for refugees.

In 1969 Bishop [Francis Hsu](/wiki/Francis_Hsu "Francis Hsu") became, after the resignation of [Lorenzo Bianchi](/wiki/Lorenzo_Bianchi "Lorenzo Bianchi"), the first ethnically Chinese bishop of Hong Kong.

On 29 May 1988 [John Baptist Wu](/wiki/John_Baptist_Wu "John Baptist Wu"), the fifth bishop, was named a member of the [College of Cardinals](/wiki/College_of_Cardinals "College of Cardinals") by [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II"). He was the first [cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_(Catholicism) "Cardinal (Catholicism)") from the Hong Kong diocese.

On 18 August 1991, an Open Forum on "Elections 1991", jointly organised by the Council of Priests, the Justice and Peace Commission, the Central Council of Catholic Laity and the Catholic Institute for Religion and Society, was held in the nine constituencies of Hong Kong, Kowloon and the New Territories in order to encourage the faithful to take an active part in the direct elections to the Legislative Council on 15 September. Church organisations also made a similar appeal to the faithful and ordinary citizens through publications, questionnaires and advertisements in newspapers.

On 15 April 1993 the diocese was re-divided into nine [deaneries](/wiki/Deaneries "Deaneries"). The Council of Priests was reorganised with all the deans included as ex officio members.

After the death of Cardinal Wu on 23 September 2002, his [coadjutor](/wiki/Coadjutor_bishop "Coadjutor bishop") [Joseph Zen Ze-kiun](/wiki/Joseph_Zen_Ze-kiun "Joseph Zen Ze-kiun") became the 6th bishop of Hong Kong.

On 8 July 2004 the [Legislative Council](/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_Hong_Kong "Legislative Council of Hong Kong") passed the [Education (Amendment) Bill 2002](/wiki/School-Based_Management_Policy#Education_(Amendment)_Bill_2002 "School-Based Management Policy") [[yue](https://zh-yue.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%A0%A1%E6%9C%AC%E6%A2%9D%E4%BE%8B "yue:校本條例")]. Under the amended ordinance, which would be effective on 1 January 2005, every aided school would be required before 2010 to form an incorporated management committee (IMC) whose members should include elected representatives of teachers, parents of students and alumni, as well as other independent persons, with a view to promoting a school-based management. It was the concern of the Church that, as a sponsoring body, she would no longer be empowered in the future to supervise the schools under her sponsorship, nor be able to achieve her goals and objectives in Catholic education. On 5 June 2005, Zen announced that, if the Legislative Council passed the donation to support schools to create incorporated management committees on 8 July 2005, he would appeal against the decision to the court. After the Government decided to give up some of its main objectives and the diocese subsequently decided to support the motion.

On 22 February 2006, [Pope Benedict XVI](/wiki/Pope_Benedict_XVI "Pope Benedict XVI") announced that Bishop [Joseph Zen](/wiki/Joseph_Zen "Joseph Zen") would be raised to the College of Cardinals.[[14]](#cite_note-14) He was made a cardinal at a consistory held on 24 March.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Zen was an outspoken supporter of democracy and a critic of the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China"). His views on government policies were often at odds with those of Hong Kong's former [Chief Executive](/wiki/Chief_Executive_of_Hong_Kong "Chief Executive of Hong Kong") [Donald Tsang Yam-kuen](/wiki/Donald_Tsang_Yam-kuen "Donald Tsang Yam-kuen"), who is also a Catholic.

On 15 April 2009. [Pope Benedict XVI](/wiki/Pope_Benedict_XVI "Pope Benedict XVI") appointed coadjutor Bishop John Tong Hon as the 7th bishop of the Diocese of Hong Kong after Zen's resignation. Tong was named a member of the College of Cardinals on 19 February 2012. On 1 August 2017, [Pope Francis](/wiki/Pope_Francis "Pope Francis") accepted Tong's resignation, which allowed coadjutor Bishop Michael Yeung Ming Cheung to succeed him as the 8th bishop of the Diocese of Hong Kong. Due to Yeung's unexpected death on 3 January 2019, the *de facto* practice after the [handover](/wiki/Handover_of_Hong_Kong "Handover of Hong Kong") of having a coadjutor bishop succeed an outgoing bishop could not be followed. His death was shortly followed by the [Anti-Extradition Law Amendment Bill Movement](/wiki/2019%E2%80%932020_Hong_Kong_protests "2019–2020 Hong Kong protests"), which sharply divided the people of Hong Kong, including Catholics. These circumstances made it difficult to appoint a bishop that could pastorally navigate the social climate of the time, and it was not until May 2021 that Pope Francis was finally able to appoint Jesuit priest Stephen Chow as the 9th bishop of Hong Kong.

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Hong_Kong&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Politics")]

Traditionally, Catholics in Hong Kong tend to support the [pro-democracy camp](/wiki/Pro-democracy_camp_(Hong_Kong) "Pro-democracy camp (Hong Kong)"), despite this there are also many Catholics that support the [pro-Beijing camp](/wiki/Pro-Beijing_camp_(Hong_Kong) "Pro-Beijing camp (Hong Kong)"). Notably, former [Chief Executive of Hong Kong](/wiki/Chief_Executive_of_Hong_Kong "Chief Executive of Hong Kong") [Donald Tsang](/wiki/Donald_Tsang "Donald Tsang") and [Carrie Lam](/wiki/Carrie_Lam "Carrie Lam") and current [Chief Executive of Hong Kong](/wiki/Chief_Executive_of_Hong_Kong "Chief Executive of Hong Kong") [John Lee](/wiki/John_Lee_Ka-chiu "John Lee Ka-chiu") are Catholics.

In July 2022, the Vatican's unofficial representative in Hong Kong, monsignor Javier Herrera-Corona, warned that religious freedoms were over in Hong Kong due to pressure from mainland Chinese authorities, with one person summarizing the monsignor's message as "Hong Kong is not the great Catholic beachhead it was."[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In May 2023, the United States government released the 2022 Report on International Religious Freedom, which stated religious leaders reported that they were self-censoring politically sensitive content, and not appointing clergy members who were critical of the Hong Kong government.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

In May 2023, the diocese announced that it would not hold any commemorations for the [Tiananmen Square massacre](/wiki/Tiananmen_Square_massacre "Tiananmen Square massacre"), an annual tradition in Hong Kong since 1989 that was last celebrated in 2021.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Coadjutor bishops

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Hong_Kong&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Coadjutor bishops")]

Under the Code of [Canon law](/wiki/Canon_law_(Catholic_Church) "Canon law (Catholic Church)"), the [coadjutor bishop](/wiki/Coadjutor_bishop "Coadjutor bishop") has the right of succession (*cum jure successionis*) upon the death, retirement or resignation of the diocesan bishop he is assisting.[[22]](#cite_note-CIC-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) All coadjutor bishops eventually succeeded to become head of the Diocese of Hong Kong.

* [Lorenzo Bianchi](/wiki/Lorenzo_Bianchi "Lorenzo Bianchi") (21 April 1949 – 3 September 1951)
* [Joseph Zen](/wiki/Joseph_Zen "Joseph Zen") (13 September 1996 – 23 September 2002)
* [John Tong Hon](/wiki/John_Tong_Hon "John Tong Hon") (30 January 2008 – 15 April 2009)
* [Michael Yeung](/wiki/Michael_Yeung "Michael Yeung") (13 November 2016 – 1 August 2017)

### Auxiliary bishops

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Hong_Kong&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Auxiliary bishops")]

Unlike coadjutors, [auxiliary bishops](/wiki/Auxiliary_bishop "Auxiliary bishop") do not have the right of succession, per canon 975, §1 of the [1983 Code of Canon Law](/wiki/1983_Code_of_Canon_Law "1983 Code of Canon Law").[[22]](#cite_note-CIC-22) Hsu, Lei, Tong, and Yeung went on to become Bishop of Hong Kong.

* [Francis Hsu](/wiki/Francis_Hsu "Francis Hsu") (1 July 1967 – 30 November 1968)
* [Peter Lei](/wiki/Peter_Lei "Peter Lei") (3 July 1971 – 8 September 1971)
* [John Tong](/wiki/John_Tong "John Tong") (13 September 1996 – 30 January 2008)
* [Michael Yeung](/wiki/Michael_Yeung "Michael Yeung") (11 July 2014 – 13 November 2016)
* [Stephen Lee](/wiki/Stephen_Lee_(bishop) "Stephen Lee (bishop)") (11 July 2014 – 23 January 2016)
* [Joseph Ha](/wiki/Joseph_Ha "Joseph Ha") (11 July 2014 – Present)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Hong_Kong&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [List of Catholic schools in Hong Kong](/wiki/List_of_Catholic_schools_in_Hong_Kong "List of Catholic schools in Hong Kong")

In the period before the [handover of Hong Kong](/wiki/Handover_of_Hong_Kong "Handover of Hong Kong") and in the period immediately after, the diocese had what the *[South China Morning Post](/wiki/South_China_Morning_Post "South China Morning Post")* referred to as "tight control" over the diocesan-operated schools.[[26]](#cite_note-SCMPdice-26) The Hong Kong government had a 2004 ordinance that required each school to establish an incorporated management committee (IMC) that would manage each school. The diocese argued that this contradicted the [Hong Kong Basic Law](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Basic_Law "Hong Kong Basic Law")'s article 141 which stated that the management systems established by religious schools would continue as is.[[26]](#cite_note-SCMPdice-26)
